The Doctor himself died with Matt Smith. It was the end of his regeneration cycle. The Valeyard prolonged his life a bit longer when the Time Lords fused him with the Doctor on Trenzalore but also turned him into a smug, manipulative, narcissistic sociopath who had to learn the basics of being the Doctor all over again from scratch, as if he'd learned nothing from 12 previous lifetimes.

I just wish the late Michael Jayston, may he rest in peace, got one last hurrah to make that official. They should have shown an ageing Valeyard on the other side of the Time Crack making a deal with Rassilon (preferably Timothy Dalton's version) to get Gallifrey out of the pocket universe by giving him the Doctor's second regeneration cycle, only for Rassilon's plan to backfire on him in Hell Bent. It would be a great bit of fan service for Classic Who fans, allow Jayston to get a moment to shine in New Who before he died, and explain why Peter Capaldi was stomping all over "never cruel or cowardly" whilst expecting Clara Oswald to put up with his behaviour.

replies.author: @ShanghaiRooster
replies.text: @misterbween There are other factors too. TV ownership in the early 60s was somewhere above 75%. Figures  tell that the BBC sold 12.8 million licences in the year to March 1964, compared to around 24 million in the year to March 2024. In 1963 Doctor Who was a completely unknown quantity - some of the cast would have been familiar to viewers of the time from their previous work in film and TV, but you'd still need to sell this new idea. That's not so with Ncuti's full debut, which went out on Christmas Day (a pretty captive audience), but as mentioned above got fewer viewers than Antiques Roadshow and the King's Xmas Message.

replies.author: @johnnhoj6749
replies.text:
@ShanghaiRooster True, but Dr Who was also originally seen as a children's show in an early evening slot. Very few adults would have deliberately tuned in to watch this unknown kids show. If they caught the original first broadcast at all it was probably because they had children who wanted to watch it or the TV was still on the same channel from previous programmes they had watched. That admittedly was far more common than now as there were only two channels and remote controls wouldn't be common in Britain until the 1980s.
Now, in theory, Dr Who is a family show aimed at a very wide audience.

replies.author: @ShanghaiRooster
replies.text:
@johnnhoj6749 To be honest, I'm not sure The BBC ever saw Doctor Who as purely a children's show, as opposed to a show that might have appeal to children (prevailing attitudes to science fiction at the time saw it as stuff for kids, despite adult themed programmes such as The Twilight Zone, The Outer Limits, and the BBC's own Quatermass serials from the 1950s and later on Out Of The Unknown). That attitude continued right into the 80s where the original Battlestar Galactica was obviously aimed at kids first (the child character and his 'robot dog') even with the underlying genocidal story in there.

Doctor Who was made by The BBC Drama Department, not the Children's Department (which would have given it a very different tone, maybe closer to that of the Cushing films). That annoyed the latter, who thought the Saturday tea-time slot was their preserve. There may well have been some hang-over from the audience. Grandstand was the main attraction from morning through to just after Five O'Clock in the afternoon, followed by something such as a programme of cartoons or the Pink Panther TV show (I started watching Doctor Who in Pertwee's time, so this is from memories of back then). I can't recall whether the early evening news came on straight after the sport or not until after Doctor Who.

author: @diviningdragon
text: Culture, I consider this a dead franchise.  Doctor Who went through a wilderness period between 1990 and 2005.  It had a robust fan base who loved the property regardless of the hiatus, and that affection was tangible and marketable.  It could be revived under the right conditions because there was fan goodwill.  Fan capital, if you like.  The difference this time is that the fan affection for the show has been squandered for identity politics.  First Whitaker's run, , then Tennant Mark II, and now Gatwa's.  Peak viewing over a decade ago was over ten million.  Base viewing under Whitaker reached a nadir of three million.  Curiosity viewing during the 60th anniversary specials was weak--and the stories delivered by RTD were weaker.  It is probable that Disney will reconsider continued funding if Doctor Who is a net loss for them.  They are struggling.  And the BBC, which had looked at cancelling Doctor Who after Whitaker, is likely losing the licensing fees that keep it operational in the next couple of years.  Under those conditions, a second hiatus lasting a decade or more is likely.  But worse, since fans have left the IP, there may not be enough grassroots support for a revival in 2040.  Under these conditions, I consider the franchise dead.  At least, it is a dead man walking.  How many steps it takes before burial is the only question.  Correct me if I'm wrong.

replies.author: @mayotango1317
replies.text: Please, Doctor Who always had political message.

replies.author: @diviningdragon
replies.text: @mayotango1317 This is correct.  Most science fiction enjoys progressive messaging.  It only becomes an issue when it is used ‘excessively’ and without finesse.  The practice of shoehorning political messaging into the narrative at the expense of canon, characterization, and story-telling reduces established fan-bases on which entertainment like Doctor Who rely for longevity.  Therein lies the problem:  not that Doctor Who has messaging, but that it is done poorly, and at the expense of the story.

How bits are slowly filled in to create the picture of who his is over the Troughton and Pertwee eras. Then when we go back to Gallifrey for the Deadly Assassin you get to see that the Time Lords are not infallible, but are still set in their ways, as all hierarchical societies would become. When you look at how the fourth Doctor is at this point in the characters history its understandable why he would reject that, and run off into the universe to see and live it rather than watching from afar. To throw all of that away as the basis for your character is truly shocking. Look at the 50th special, which was fantastic. You see more of the Doctors childhood and one event he has sort of mentioned a few times in the past. About going outside the capitol and meeting an old man that lives in the mountains who told him stories...and there it is on screen, this time fleshed out. Its not the old man we get to see here, but the foundation of his character, of his curiosity and rebellion etc. It was a brilliant idea to work it into the 50th celebration of the show, going back to the foundation of who he is. And ten years later by the 60th all that has been thrown away, binned and he is now a she from somewhere else. Which contradicts what we have seen in the show for fifty odd years, not just once but on numerous occasions. And we get the lecture of why he is not still a woman as they know everything and he knows nothing...I dont know why they did not just pour petrol over him and set him alight. 
So RTD will continue with his queer as folk Doctor and will double down on this character change, and long term fans and people with more than one brain cell will continue to ignore what used to be a brilliant series with a compelling hero.

TV ownership in Britain circa 1960 was around 75%. Figures I saw suggests that first Hartnell episode got around 9% of the viewing audience.

Although that first story wasn't the earth-shattering event that The Daleks provided, boosting the audience to over ten million, the viewership it received - going out at the same time as JFK's assassination was dominating everything was more than respectable. Had Doctor Who's first season been of the calibre of current year output, I doubt it would have lasted beyond one or two seasons.

author: @johnnhoj6749
text:
You mention rising viewing figures in the Hartnell era. It's crucial to remember that the show was very much seen by the BBC at the time as a children's show - one with an educational angle and made by people who cared about quality, but still a kid's show. As such it was naturally shown in an early evening "teatime" slot but I think it also benefited greatly from being on a Saturday. Many adults would be at home, when they might not have been back from work on a weekday, and already watching TV as Dr Who closely followed sports results programmes. In the days before remote controls, and especially when there were only 2 UK channels then,  the TV usually passively stayed on the same channel for much longer, often all evening, than would normally be the case now.
This context meant that many more adults, and older teenagers, were exposed to the programme than would normally be the case for a children's show even then, certainly more than now.

I don't think Jodie's appearance was the first step down hill.  It had been going downhill for ages before that.  I stopped watching Capaldi's doctor as they were progressively taking the doctor down, inch by inch, line by line.

Yet before that it had drastically shifted too, because it was no longer focused on the Doctor but the companions.  It was regularly implying that human values were superior to the Doctor's evaluation of life.  Something that was drastically different to older iterations of the character and story.  The Doctor is more like the thinking man's superman armed with a screwdriver and smarts rather than muscles.  Humans should be humble in the presence of the Doctor.  He is not one of them but vastly superior.  All that is gone now except where they want to use the Doctor as an cultural reeducation tool for the masses.

I'll go back to thinking about the Doctor as a concept rather than what the writers have done with the on screen character with that title.  Ideas, as they say, are bulletproof.

author: @mistie710
text:
My personal feeling about all this is that I don't care that a woman was used to replace the Doctor at the end of Peter Capaldi's tenure, and I don't care if the current Doctor is black. Let's face it, the same could be said for Eccleston who played the Doctor as a northener. However the difference was that Eccleston played in some epic stories that captured the imagination of so many people  and to a great extent stayed away from politics and sex. 

That's not to say that Who has stayed clear of such things. Indeed I recall one of Sylvester McCoy's stories having a heavy anti-racist undertone, not to mention that Capaldi journeyed with a self-confessed lesbian (and did she ever confess it!) The problem to my mind is that Gatwa and Whittaker are featured in some of the most divisive and political stories in the entirety of Who's existence. Whilst Whittaker seemed to be quiet on the subject at the time, Gatwa is quite vocal about his sexuality and insists on parading it in and out of character which kills any interest in the actual story, what there is of it. It's also the reason why I now have my doubts about Tennant after that blatant attempt to boost viewing figures by bringing him back only to blast him and his time as the Doctor with pseudo-feminist rhetoric before yet again trying to rewrite the lore of the series.

Of course Who is dead. Since before the removal of Capaldi, the show has been moving away from its roots and its lore, not to mention the bait-and-switch antics of Russell T. Davies and the involvement of Disney, so alienating those that were fans of the Classic series; the story quality has been declining rapidly, hence alienating the more recent fans who joined in when Chris Eccleston was in the hotseat and new fans are few and far between, mostly because the sort of people that Gatwa & Co. appeal to are a minority. Not a big minority either.

My view is that it isn't the sex or the skin that killed Who, but it is the insistence that sex and race are more important than good storylines. If Gatwa and his cronies don't like it, then they can go touch their own grass.
